chunk_summary: Rehabilitation in SAM includes sensory stimulation and emotional support to improve neurodevelopment, caregiver engagement, and recovery outcomes, alongside therapeutic feeding.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of sensory stimulation and emotional support in SAM rehabilitation.
Learn how to create structured daily routines for caregivers and children during SAM recovery.
Identify and address barriers to recovery, including maternal depression and household food insecurity.
Recognize early markers of recovery, such as improved playfulness and interaction.
key_facts: Sensory stimulation and emotional support should begin once the child is clinically stable.
Structured daily routines include responsive feeding, short play sessions (15–30 minutes, at least twice daily), and safe mobility.
Improving playfulness is an early marker of recovery and should be documented alongside intake and weight gain.
Barriers to recovery include maternal depression, food insecurity, domestic violence, and feeding problems related to disability.
Deterioration in activity is a danger sign, even if weight has not yet fallen.
reference_quote: Treat sensory stimulation and emotional support as a prescribed intervention that begins once the child is clinically stable. (Source: WHO, Pocket book of hospital care for children)
